description: The zebra shark is a distinctive, slow-moving species found on coral reefs and sandy seafloors of the tropical Indo-Pacific. Adults are covered in small dark spots on a pale background, though juveniles bear striking black-and-white stripes — giving the species its common name. It is primarily nocturnal, hunting mollusks, crustaceans, and small fish by probing crevices with its flexible body.
funFact: Juvenile zebra sharks are born with bold black-and-white stripes that gradually break up into the adult's spotted pattern over their first few years — making young and adult animals look so different they were once formally described as separate species.
habitat: Shallow coral reefs, sandy flats, and rocky seafloors in tropical Indo-Pacific waters from the Red Sea and East Africa to Japan and Australia. Typically found at depths of 0–62 m, often resting motionless on the bottom during the day in areas with strong currents.
geographicRangeDescription: Found throughout the tropical Indo-Pacific, from the Red Sea and the coasts of East Africa and the Persian Gulf east through South and Southeast Asia to Japan, the Philippines, and northern and eastern Australia. Range extends into the western Pacific as far as Palau and parts of Melanesia. Most abundant around the coral reefs and sandy lagoons of the Indian Ocean and the Coral Triangle.
diet: A specialized benthic predator feeding primarily on mollusks, crustaceans, small bony fish, and sea snakes. Uses its flexible, elongated body to squeeze into reef crevices and extract prey, and its strong, pavement-like teeth to crush hard-shelled invertebrates.
size: Typically 1.5–2.5 m (4.9–8.2 ft); max confirmed ~3.5 m (11.5 ft) including the long caudal fin. Males and females are similar in size.

vitalSigns.label: Estimated Population
vitalSigns.value: Unknown — severely depleted in large portions of its Indo-Pacific range

vitalSigns.label: Reproductive Rate
vitalSigns.value: Up to 4 eggs per clutch (oviparous); multiple clutches per year possible; eggs hatch after ~170 days

vitalSigns.label: Diet
vitalSigns.value: Mollusks, crustaceans, small bony fish, sea snakes

vitalSigns.label: Population Growth Rate
vitalSigns.value: Low to moderate — egg-laying reproduction and earlier maturity give slightly better recovery potential than viviparous species, but habitat dependency limits resilience

vitalSigns.label: Key Adaptations
vitalSigns.value: Highly flexible body for navigating reef crevices; pavement-like crushing teeth for hard-shelled prey; can pump water over gills while resting motionless on the seafloor

habitatStats.label: Global Range
habitatStats.value: Tropical Indo-Pacific — Red Sea, East Africa, South Asia, Southeast Asia, northern Australia, and Japan; associated with coral reefs and sandy coastal habitats

habitatStats.label: Depth Range
habitatStats.value: 0–62 m (0–203 ft); most common in shallow reef zones under 30 m

threats.name: Coral Reef Degradation
threats.description: Entirely dependent on coral reef and sandy reef-flat habitats throughout its range. Bleaching events, destructive fishing, and coastal development have severely degraded reefs across Southeast Asia, South Asia, and the Red Sea — directly eliminating habitat and prey.

threats.name: Overfishing and Bycatch
threats.description: Heavily targeted by artisanal and commercial fisheries across Southeast Asia for meat and fins. Also caught in large numbers as bycatch in coastal trawls, gillnets, and hook-and-line fisheries. Bottom trawling in particular is devastating in shallow reef habitats where this species lives.

threats.name: Aquarium Trade
threats.description: Its striking appearance makes the zebra shark popular in the global aquarium trade. Wild collection for display, particularly of juveniles, adds additional pressure on already-depleted reef populations in parts of its range.

threats.name: Habitat Specificity
threats.description: Unlike wide-ranging pelagic sharks, the zebra shark is tied to specific shallow reef systems. This makes populations highly fragmented and unable to recolonize degraded reefs from elsewhere once locally extirpated.

threats.name: Climate Change
threats.description: Coral bleaching driven by ocean warming directly destroys the reef habitat zebra sharks depend on. Projected losses of coral reef cover in the Indo-Pacific under current warming scenarios pose a severe long-term threat to the species.

actionItems.title: Support Coral Reef Marine Protected Areas
actionItems.description: Zebra sharks depend entirely on healthy coral reefs. Advocate for the expansion and enforcement of MPAs covering reef habitats across Southeast Asia, the Red Sea, and Australia — the core of this species' range.

actionItems.title: End Destructive Bottom Trawling
actionItems.description: Bottom trawling in shallow reef zones is one of the most destructive forces for zebra shark habitat and population. Support campaigns and policy efforts to ban trawling in reef and coastal habitats within the species' Indo-Pacific range.

actionItems.title: Choose Reef-Friendly Aquarium Products
actionItems.description: If purchasing marine aquarium animals or products, verify they are captive-bred rather than wild-caught. Zebra sharks are bred in captivity — captive-bred specimens reduce pressure on wild reef populations.

actionItems.title: Support the IUCN SSG and Reef Programs
actionItems.description: Donate to organizations funding reef shark monitoring and conservation programs across Southeast Asia — the region where zebra shark declines have been most severe and where data is most urgently needed.

healthMetrics.label: Population Trend
healthMetrics.value: Decreasing — severely depleted in Southeast Asia and South Asia

healthMetrics.label: Habitat Quality
healthMetrics.value: Poor and declining — coral reefs degrading across the Indo-Pacific

healthMetrics.label: Prey Availability
healthMetrics.value: Declining — reef invertebrate and fish communities degraded by bleaching and fishing

healthMetrics.label: Fishing Pressure
healthMetrics.value: High — targeted and bycatch in coastal fisheries; bottom trawling devastating reefs

healthMetrics.label: Protection Coverage
healthMetrics.value: Limited — some national protections; spotty MPA coverage; no CITES listing

populationTrendMeta.note: Rough global estimates modeled from IUCN-documented decline of >50% over three generations, driven primarily by reef degradation and fishing pressure in Southeast Asia. No formal stock assessment exists. Figures carry high uncertainty.

preyDeclineRegions.name: SE Asia
preyDeclineRegions.note: Reef invertebrates and fish severely depleted by blast fishing, trawling, and bleaching

preyDeclineRegions.name: South Asia
preyDeclineRegions.note: Coastal reef systems heavily degraded; mollusks and crustaceans overharvested

preyDeclineRegions.name: Red Sea
preyDeclineRegions.note: Coral bleaching and coastal development reducing reef prey availability

preyDeclineRegions.name: Australia
preyDeclineRegions.note: GBR bleaching events reducing reef complexity and benthic prey abundance

preyDeclineRegions.name: East Africa
preyDeclineRegions.note: Artisanal fishing pressure on reef invertebrates; some areas well-managed

fishingPressureRegions.name: SE Asia
fishingPressureRegions.note: Targeted for meat and fins; blast fishing destroys reef habitat and kills sharks directly

fishingPressureRegions.name: South Asia
fishingPressureRegions.note: Caught in coastal trawls and gillnets; minimal species-specific protections

fishingPressureRegions.name: Red Sea
fishingPressureRegions.note: Artisanal and commercial fishing pressure; no zebra shark-specific restrictions

fishingPressureRegions.name: East Africa
fishingPressureRegions.note: Targeted in some artisanal fisheries; patchy MPA coverage provides limited refuge

fishingPressureRegions.name: Australia
fishingPressureRegions.note: Protected in Australian waters; strong MPA network provides meaningful refuge
